content:
{
  "title": "Adaptive Counter-Prompting: Dynamic Adversarial Checkpointing for Robust LLM Responses",
  "problem": "Large language models remain vulnerable to adversarial prompts and jailbreak attacks that bypass safety systems. Existing prompting strategies often fail to adapt in real-time to subtle attack variations.",
  "motivation": "As LLMs are increasingly deployed in safety-critical and open-ended settings, robustness to adversarial jailbreaking is paramount. Recent attacks show that static prompt-based defenses are easily circumvented, highlighting the need for more adaptive and context-aware methods.",
  "method": "We propose Adaptive Counter-Prompting (ACP), a novel prompting strategy that interleaves the user's input with dynamically generated counter-prompts at inference time. The LLM, prior to answering, is prompted to self-critique or simulate an adversarial user, generating possible malicious reinterpretations of the input. The model then conditions its final answer on both the original prompt and its own simulated adversarial perspective, aiming to neutralize potentially unsafe completions. This approach leverages the LLM's in-context learning ability to adversarially 'test' its own outputs during inference, dynamically adapting to unseen attack patterns.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate ACP on established adversarial jailbreak datasets (e.g., AdvBench, jailbreak prompts from public red-teaming). Baselines will include standard safety prompts and chain-of-thought self-critique. Metrics include attack success rate reduction, response helpfulness, and safety compliance, as judged by both automated classifiers and human raters."
}
